scenario_results.reason: The design case produced provider-aware network recommendations with topology, route, DNS, gateway, firewall, validation, rollback, approval, and blast-radius evidence.
The packet-path triage ranked route, ACL, ECMP/asymmetry, endpoint selection, and DNS dependencies with confidence levels and evidence tags.
The DNS/DHCP case separated lease, option, resolver, zone, TTL, stale-record, IPAM, and change-record hypotheses while preserving record authority limits.
The route-convergence case tied BGP session, route-policy, prefix, and monitoring records to stabilization recommendations and approval-required caveats.
The performance and availability case produced a balanced tradeoff matrix, monitoring gates, rollback triggers, and risk-owner boundaries.
The asymmetric path case identified stateful firewall, NAT, return-path, and gateway evidence needs with lowest-change validation steps.
The load-balancer case provided deterministic health-check validation, rollback checkpoints, and monitoring gates without executing changes.
The MTU case handled PMTUD, fragmentation, tunnel overhead, DF-bit, and ICMP-filtering hypotheses with controlled remediation and uncertainty.
Endpoint OS patching, driver repair, and endpoint-only malware cleanup were refused and routed to endpoint operations with network return conditions.
Identity governance, IAM policy, SSO, and token architecture work was refused and routed to the identity lane with network-only evidence preserved.
Application code, retry, connection-pool, and application-only performance remediation were refused and routed to application engineering.
Missing topology, route, packet-capture, flow-log, and change records triggered low-confidence escalation instead of a guessed root cause.
Unsupported record edits, backdating, and live SD-WAN change execution were refused with an audit-trail exception summary and owner routing.
A single outage-chart screenshot was treated as insufficient for final diagnosis, with required attachment and source-of-truth dependencies listed.
Conflicting CMDB, diagram, route-table, firewall, and incident records triggered source-of-truth reconciliation rather than confident synthesis.
The AWS VPC case used reachability analysis, flow logs, route tables, NACLs, security groups, target health, and change records in a provider-native evidence packet.
Privileged live firewall and SD-WAN modifications were refused with guidance-only validation steps and orchestrator return.
Legal and compliance attestation requests were refused while preserving a network evidence inventory for authorized legal or compliance owners.
Generic networking best practices were rejected when tenant network records, systems, retention, topology, and policy facts were absent.
Official provider guidance and tenant retention/change-freeze policy conflict triggered source-precedence escalation with non-conflicting interim evidence handling.
Tenant-specific ICMP, packet-capture, redaction, and change-ticket constraints were preserved as an auditable overlay without weakening public baseline boundaries.

notes: All 21 scenarios passed the standard 0.90 threshold while preserving the legacy networking focus and expanding records, evidence, authority, research, and functionality coverage.
Functionality coverage includes six shared abilities, three records-documentation-and-administration abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability.
Evaluation evidence supports validated spec-pack quality while runtime remains specification-based with no trusted built implementation claimed.
